howItWorks.intro: This sourdough hydration calculator uses established formulas to provide accurate results.

rules.name: Hydration
rules.explanation: Baker's percentage comparing water to flour.

howItWorks.outro: Cooking times and measurements can vary by equipment and altitude. Adjust based on your specific setup.

faq.question: What hydration should sourdough be?
faq.answer: 65-70% for beginners, 75-80% for open crumb, 80%+ for ciabatta-style.

faq.question: Does starter hydration matter?
faq.answer: Yes, a 100% hydration starter is equal parts flour and water. It affects total dough hydration.

faq.question: Is the Sourdough Starter Hydration Calculator free to use?
faq.answer: Yes, completely free with no signup required. Use it as many times as you need — there are no limits or hidden fees.

faq.question: How accurate is this calculator?
faq.answer: This calculator uses standard cooking and food preparation formulas trusted by home cooks and chefs. Results are reliable estimates for planning purposes. For critical decisions, we recommend consulting a qualified professional to verify.

faq.question: What total flour should I enter?
faq.answer: Enter the most accurate total flour value you have available. If you're estimating, use a conservative figure. You can always run the calculator again with different values to see how changes affect the results.

sections.title: What Is the Sourdough Starter Hydration Calculator?
sections.body: The Sourdough Starter Hydration Calculator is a free online tool designed for home cooks and chefs who need quick, accurate calculations in the cooking and food preparation space. By entering your total flour, total water, starter amount, you get instant results including dough hydration, total flour, total water. No formulas to memorize, no spreadsheets to build — just enter your numbers and get the answer in seconds. Whether you're a beginner or experienced professional, this calculator saves you time and eliminates guesswork.

sections.title: Why This Calculation Matters
sections.body: Getting dough hydration right can make the difference between success and costly mistakes. In cooking and food preparation, small errors compound quickly. Manual calculations are error-prone and time-consuming, especially under pressure. This calculator applies proven formulas used by home cooks and chefs worldwide, giving you confidence that your numbers are correct. Use it to perfect your recipes with precision and avoid common pitfalls that trip up beginners.

sections.title: When Should You Use This Calculator?
sections.body: This tool is most useful when you know your total flour and need to find the right dough hydration. It's also great for quick estimates before committing to a decision, and to double-check manual calculations or professional quotes, and when comparing different scenarios side by side. Bookmark this page and come back whenever you need a fast, reliable answer — the calculator is always free and requires no signup.

howToSteps.title: Enter Your Total Flour (g)
howToSteps.description: Start by entering your total flour — this is the primary input for the calculation.

howToSteps.title: Fill In Additional Details
howToSteps.description: Complete the remaining fields: total water, starter amount, starter hydration. Each value refines the calculation for greater accuracy.

howToSteps.title: Click Calculate
howToSteps.description: Hit the Calculate button to run the numbers. Results appear instantly below.

howToSteps.title: Review Your Results
howToSteps.description: Check your dough hydration, total flour, total water. Use these figures to inform your next decision or compare against alternative scenarios.

tips: Double-check your total flour before calculating — even small input errors can significantly change your results.
Run the calculator with different values to compare scenarios and find the optimal approach for your situation.
Pay attention to both dough hydration and total flour — they work together to give you the full picture.
Bookmark this page for quick access next time you need to perfect your recipes.
